original_hypothesis.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint() can be re-entered via Treasury.rebalanceTreasury(), potentially corrupting epoch transitions and breaking epoch monotonicity
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.checkpoint performs an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before incrementing epochCounter. Tokenomics contains a _locked variable but does not use it in checkpoint(). If the Treasury is malicious/compromised or contains an unintended callback into Tokenomics.checkpoint(), it may cause nested checkpoint execution with inconsistent state (prevEpochTime/endTime/effectiveBond/maxBond) and potentially violate epochCounter monotonicity expectations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a high-impact integrity risk but low realism under the stated trust model (Treasury is trusted). Still, it is an operational hazard if Treasury is upgraded or replaced and accidentally becomes re-entrant.
deployment_assumptions: Treasury contract is upgradable or can be replaced via Tokenomics.changeManagers
Treasury.rebalanceTreasury can call back into Tokenomics.checkpoint (directly or indirectly)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Tokenomics.changeManagers allows owner to update treasury; Tokenomics.checkpoint external-calls treasury without reentrancy guard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(...) occurs before epochCounter is updated; no _locked check in checkpoint()

preconditions: Treasury.rebalanceTreasury makes a (re)entrant call into Tokenomics.checkpoint() or a path that triggers it
Epoch timing checks pass for both frames (diffNumSeconds within bounds, lastDonationBlockNumber != block.number)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reentrancy lock exists in Tokenomics but not applied to checkpoint
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires compromised/malicious treasury or unsafe upgrade

operational_questions: Does the deployed Treasury have any callback behavior into Tokenomics?
Is Treasury upgradeable and governed by the same DAO/owner?
Are there monitoring controls preventing treasury upgrades to unsafe code?
validation_requirements: Review Treasury implementation and any hooks invoked in rebalanceTreasury
Simulate reentrant Treasury in tests to see if nested checkpoint can succeed
Assess whether timestamp/diff checks prevent reentrant second settlement

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Introduce or compromise Treasury with reentrant rebalanceTreasury
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call Tokenomics.checkpoint() to trigger external call
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During rebalanceTreasury, call back into Tokenomics.checkpoint() to attempt double-settlement or state corruption
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit corrupted epoch state to misallocate incentives/effectiveBond
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control over Treasury code path

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Epoch accounting corruption; incentive misallocation; possible system halt
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High: may require governance intervention and state repair/migration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Nested checkpoint traces; unusual multiple EpochSettled events in one tx

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
